operator that implements the logic for windowing based on a {@link WindowAssigner} and {@link\\n83:  * Trigger}.\\n84:  *\\n85:  * <p>When an element arrives it gets assigned a key using a {@link KeySelector} and it gets\\n86:  * assigned to zero or more windows using a {@link WindowAssigner}. Based on this, the element is\\n87:  * put into panes. A pane is the bucket of elements that have the same key and same {@code Window}.\\n88:  * An element can be in multiple panes if it was assigned to multiple windows by the {@code\\n89:  * WindowAssigner}.\\n90:  *\\n91:  * <p>Each pane gets its own instance of the provided {@code Trigger}. This trigger determines when\\n92:  * the contents of the pane should be processed to emit results.     // element not handled by any window\\n437:         // late arriving tag has been set\\n438:         // windowAssigner is event time and current timestamp + allowed lateness no less than\\n439:         // element timestamp\\n440:         if (isSkippedElement && isElementLate(element)) {\\n441:             if (lateDataOutputTag != null) {\\n442:                 sideOutput(element);\\n443:             } else {\\n444:                 this.numLateRecordsDropped.inc();\\n445:             }\\n446:         }\\n447:     }\\n448: \\n449:     @Override\\n450:     public void onEventTime(InternalTimer<K, W> timer) throws Exception {\\n451:         triggerContext.key = timer.getKey();\\n452:         triggerContext.window = timer.getNamespace();\\n453: \\n454:         MergingWindowSet<W> mergingWindows;\\n455: \\n456:         if (windowAssigner instanceof MergingWindowAssigner) {\\n457:             mergingWindows = getMergingWindowSet();\\n458:             W @Override\\n497:     public void onProcessingTime(InternalTimer<K, W> timer) throws Exception {\\n498:         triggerContext.key = timer.getKey();\\n499:         triggerContext.window = java.util.Collection;\\n77: \\n78: import static org.apache.flink.util.Preconditions.checkArgument;\\n79: import static org.apache.flink.util.Preconditions.checkNotNull;\\n80: \\n81: /**\\n82:  * An operator that implements the logic for windowing based on a {@link WindowAssigner} and {@link\\n83:  * Trigger}.\\n84:  *\\n85:  * <p>When an element arrives it gets assigned a key using a {@link KeySelector} and it gets\\n86:  * assigned to zero or more windows using a {@link WindowAssigner}. Based on this, the element is\\n87:  * put into panes. A pane is the bucket of elements that have the same key and same {@code Window}.\\n88:  * An element can be in multiple panes if it was assigned to multiple windows by the {@code\\n89:  * WindowAssigner}.\\n90:  *\\n91:  * <p>Each pane gets its own instance of the provided {@code Trigger}. This trigger determines when\\n92:  * the contents of the pane should be processed to emit results. When a trigger fires, the given\\n93:  * {@link InternalWindowFunction} is invoked to produce the results that are emitted for the pane to\\n94:  * which the {@code Trigger} belongs.\\n95:  *\\n96:  * @param <K> The type of key returned by the {@code KeySelector}.\\n97:  * @param <IN> The type of the incoming elements.\\n98:  * @param <OUT> The type of elements emitted by the {@code InternalWindowFunction}.\\n99:  * @param <W> The type of {@code Window} that the {@code WindowAssigner} assigns.\\n100:  */\\n101: @Internal\\n102: public class WindowOperator<K, IN, ACC, OUT, W extends Window>\\n103:         extends AbstractUdfStreamOperator<OUT, InternalWindowFunction<ACC, OUT, K, W>>\\n104:         implements OneInputStreamOperator<IN, OUT>, Triggerable<K, W> {\\n105: \\n106:     private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;\\n107: \\n108:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n109:     // Configuration values and user functions\\n110:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\\n111: \\n112:     protected final WindowAssigner<? super IN, W> windowAssigner;\\n113: \\n114:     private final KeySelector<IN, K> keySelector;\\n115: \\n116:     private final Trigger<? super IN, ? super W> trigger;\\n117: \\n118:     private final StateDescriptor<? extends AppendingState<IN, ACC>, ?> windowStateDescriptor;\\n119: \\n120:     /** For serializing the key in checkpoints. */\\n121:     protected final TypeSerializer<K> keySerializer;\\n122: \\n123:     /** For serializing the window in checkpoints. */\\n124:     protected final TypeSerializer<W> windowSerializer;\\n125: \\n126:     /**\\n127:      * The allowed lateness for elements. \\n292:     @Override\\n293:     public void processElement(StreamRecord<IN> element) throws Exception {\\n294:         final Collection<W> elementWindows =\\n295:     }\\n349: \\n350:                                         triggerContext.key = key;\\n351:                                         triggerContext.window = mergeResult;\\n352: \\n353:                            }\\n366:                                 });\\n367: \\n368:                 // drop if the window is already late\\n369:                 if (isWindowLate(actualWindow))      } else {\\n444:                 this.numLateRecordsDropped.inc();\\n445:             }\\n446:         }\\n447:     }\\n448: \\n449:     @Override\\n450:     public void .setOutputOnlyAfterEndOfStream(isOutputOnlyAfterEndOfStream)\\n550:                 .build();\\n551:     }\\n552: \\n553:     /**\\n554:      * Drops all state for the given window and calls {@link lateness of\\n607:      * the given window.\\n608:      */\\n609:     protected boolean isWindowLate(W window) {\\n610:         return (windowAssigner.isEventTime()\\n611:                 && (cleanupTime(window) <= internalTimerService.currentWatermark()));\\n612:     }\\n613: \\n614:     /**\\n615:      * Decide if a record is currently late, based on current watermark and allowed lateness.\\n616:      *\\n617:      * @param element The element to check\\n618:      * @return The element for which should be considered when sideoutputs\\n619:      */\\n620:     protected boolean isElementLate(StreamRecord<IN> element) {\\n621:         return (windowAssigner.isEventTime())\\n622:                 && (element.getTimestamp() + allowedLateness\\n623:                         <= internalTimerService.currentWatermark());\\n624:     }\\n625: \\n626:     /**\\n627:      * Registers a timer to cleanup the content of the window.\\n628:      *\\n629:      * @param window the window whose state to discard\\n630:      */\\n631:     protected void registerCleanupTimer(W window) {\\n632:         long cleanupTime = cleanupTime(window);\\n633:         if (cleanupTime == Long.MAX_VALUE) {\\n634:             // don't set a GC timer for \\\"end of time\\\"\\n635:             return;\\n636:         }\\n637: \\n638:         if (windowAssigner.isEventTime()) {\\n639:             triggerContext.registerEventTimeTimer(cleanupTime);\\n640:         } else {\\n641:             triggerContext.registerProcessingTimeTimer(cleanupTime);\\n642:         }\\n643:     }\\n644: \\n645:     /**\\n646:      * Deletes the cleanup timer set for the contents of the provided window.\\n647:      *\\n648:      * @param window the window whose state to discard\\n649:      */\\n650:     protected void deleteCleanupTimer(W window) {\\n651:         long cleanupTime = cleanupTime(window);\\n652:         if (cleanupTime == Long.MAX_VALUE) {\\n653:             // no need to clean up because we didn't set one\\n654:             return;\\n655:         }\\n656:         if (windowAssigner.isEventTime()) {\\n657:             triggerContext.deleteEventTimeTimer(cleanupTime);\\n658:         } else {\\n659:             triggerContext.deleteProcessingTimeTimer(cleanupTime);\\n660:         }\\n661:     }\\n662: \\n663:     /**\\n664:      * Returns the cleanup time for a window, which is {@code window.maxTimestamp +\\n665:      * allowedLateness}. In case this leads to a value greater than {@link Long#MAX_VALUE} then a\\n666:      * cleanup time of {@link Long#MAX_VALUE} is returned.\\n667:      *\\n668:      * @param window the window whose cleanup time we are computing.\\n669:      */\\n670:     private long cleanupTime(W window) {\\n671:         if (windowAssigner.isEventTime()) {\\n672:             long cleanupTime = window.maxTimestamp() + allowedLateness;\\n673:             return cleanupTime >= window.maxTimestamp() ? cleanupTime : Long.MAX_VALUE;\\n674:         } else {\\n675:             return window.maxTimestamp();\\n676:         }\\n677:     }\\n678: \\n679:     /** Returns {@code true} if the given time is the cleanup time for the given window. */\\n680:     protected final boolean isCleanupTime(W window, long time) {\\n681:         return time == cleanupTime(window);\\n682:     }\\n683: \\n684:     /**\\n685:      * Base class for per-window {@link KeyedStateStore KeyedStateStores}. Used to allow per-window\\n686:      * state access for {@link\\n687:      * org.apache.flink.streaming.api.functions.windowing.ProcessWindowFunction}.\\n688:      */\\n689:     public abstract class MergingWindowStateStore extends AbstractPerWindowStateStore {\\n714: \\n715:         public MergingWindowStateStore(\\n716:                 KeyedStateBackend<?> keyedStateBackend, ExecutionConfig java.util.Collection;\n77: \n78: import static org.apache.flink.util.Preconditions.checkArgument;\n79: import static org.apache.flink.util.Preconditions.checkNotNull;\n80: \n81: /**\n82:  * An operator that implements the logic for windowing based on a {@link WindowAssigner} and {@link\n83:  * Trigger}.\n84:  *\n85:  * <p>When an element arrives it gets assigned a key using a {@link KeySelector} and it gets\n86:  * assigned to zero or more windows using a {@link WindowAssigner}. Based on this, the element is\n87:  * put into panes. A pane is the bucket of elements that have the same key and same {@code Window}.\n88:  * An element can be in multiple panes if it was assigned to multiple windows by the {@code\n89:  * WindowAssigner}.\n90:  *\n91:  * <p>Each pane gets its own instance of the provided {@code Trigger}. This trigger determines when\n92:  * the contents of the pane should be processed to emit results. When a trigger fires, the given\n93:  * {@link InternalWindowFunction} is invoked to produce the results that are emitted for the pane to\n94:  * which the {@code Trigger} belongs.\n95:  *\n96:  * @param <K> The type of key returned by the {@code KeySelector}.\n97:  * @param <IN> The type of the incoming elements.\n98:  * @param <OUT> The type of elements emitted by the {@code InternalWindowFunction}.\n99:  * @param <W> The type of {@code Window} that the {@code WindowAssigner} assigns.\n100:  */\n101: @Internal\n102: public class WindowOperator<K, IN, ACC, OUT, W extends Window>\n103:         extends AbstractUdfStreamOperator<OUT, InternalWindowFunction<ACC, OUT, K, W>>\n104:         implements OneInputStreamOperator<IN, OUT>, Triggerable<K, W> {\n105: \n106:     private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;\n107: \n108:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\n109:     // Configuration values and user functions\n110:     // ------------------------------------------------------------------------\n111: \n112:     protected final WindowAssigner<? super IN, W> windowAssigner;\n113: \n114:     private final KeySelector<IN, K> keySelector;\n115: \n116:     private final Trigger<? super IN, ? super W> trigger;\n117: \n118:     private final StateDescriptor<? extends AppendingState<IN, ACC>, ?> windowStateDescriptor;\n119: \n120:     /** For serializing the key in checkpoints. */\n121:     protected final TypeSerializer<K> keySerializer;\n122: \n123:     /** For serializing the window in checkpoints. */\n124:     protected final TypeSerializer<W> windowSerializer;\n125: \n126:     /**\n127:      * The allowed lateness for elements. This is used for:\n128:      *\n129:      * <ul>\n130:      *   <li>Deciding if an element should be dropped from a window due to lateness.\n131:      *   <li>Clearing the state of a window if the system time passes the {@code window.maxTimestamp\n132:      *       + allowedLateness} landmark.\n133:      * </ul>\n134:      */\n135:     protected final long allowedLateness;\n136: \n137:     /**\n138:      * {@link OutputTag} to use for late arriving events. \n182:     /** Creates a new {@code WindowOperator} based on the given policies and user functions. */\n183:     public WindowOperator(\n184:             WindowAssigner<? super IN, W> state to discard\n630:      */\n631:     protected void registerCleanupTimer(W window) {\n632:         long cleanupTime = cleanupTime(window);\n633:         if (cleanupTime == Long.MAX_VALUE) {\n634: /**\n646:      * Deletes the cleanup timer set for the contents of the provided window.\n647:      *\n648:      * @param window the window whose state to discard\n649:      */\n650:     protected void +\n665:      * allowedLateness}. For session windows specifically, an element within the allowed lateness period might extend a window that would otherwise be considered late, but the current logic discards it anyway. A concrete scenario would be a session window with a 5-minute gap and allowedLateness of 0—an element arriving just after the watermark could still merge into an existing session, but the code marks it as late based on the current window state rather than the potential merged state. the element is actually late since its timestamp is 10:11, which is after the watermark of 10:10. The key insight is that the element's timestamp is past the watermark, not before it. So the element isn't late in that scenario. Let me work through a case where it actually is late: with a session window (5-minute gap), allowedLateness = 0, an existing window [10:00 - 10:05], and an element arriving at watermark 10:10 with timestamp 10:09. Since `10:09 + 0 <= 10:10` is true, the element qualifies as late. Even if it merges with the existing window to create [10:00 - 10:09], that merged window would also fail the lateness check for the same reason.\n\nIn this case, the element gets sent to the side output. That behavior makes sense when allowedLateness is 0—anything older than the watermark should be treated as late. But looking back at the bug report, there's a deeper issue: when an element arrives past the watermark and could merge into an existing session window that hasn't been cleaned up yet, the late element handling tries to output it to a side output, but the output collector may not have that tag registered, causing the element to silently disappear.\n\nThe `sideOutput()` method just calls `output.collect(lateDataOutputTag, element)`, so if the tag isn't properly registered with the output, the element gets dropped without any error. This suggests the problem isn't a null tag, but rather that the output object itself wasn't initialized with the tag in the first place—likely a configuration issue during job setup rather than a bug in the window operator itself.\n\nLooking back at the bug description though, the real issue is different: the lateness check happens before the merge attempt in the merging window operator's `processElement()` method. For merging windows, the code first calls `mergingWindows.addWindow()` to add and potentially merge the window, then checks `isWindowLate(actualWindow)` on the result. The problem is that when an element's timestamp is old, the merge operation might not behave as expected, causing elements that could legitimately extend a session window to be incorrectly marked as late.\n\nThe fix requires checking whether the element can merge into an existing non-expired window before classifying it as late, or ensuring the side output path properly emits late elements through the registered OutputTag.\n\nLooking at the loop, `addWindow()` creates a new window and attempts merging, but if that resulting window is late, the element gets skipped entirely. Elements from the state windows of merged\\n42:  * windows must be merged into this one state window. We keep a mapping from in-flight window to\\n43:  * state window that can be queried using {@link #getStateWindow(Window)}.\\n44:  *\\n45:  * <p>A new window can be added to the set of in-flight windows using {@link #addWindow(Window,\\n46:  * MergeFunction)}. This might merge other windows and the caller must react accordingly in the\\n47:  * {@link MergeFunction#merge(Object, Collection, Object, Collection)} and adjust the outside view\\n48:  * of windows and state.\\n49:  *\\n50:  * <p>Windows can be removed from the set of windows using {@link #retireWindow(Window)}.\\n51:  *\\n52:  * @param <W> The type of {@code Window} that this set is keeping track of.\\n53:  */\\n54: public class MergingWindowSet<W extends Window> {\\n55: \\n56:     private static final Logger LOG = LoggerFactory.getLogger(MergingWindowSet.class);\\n57: \\n58:     /**\\n59:      * Mapping from window to the window that keeps the window state. When we are incrementally\\n60:      * merging windows starting from some window we keep that starting window as the state window to\\n61:      * prevent costly state juggling.\\n62:      */\\n63:     private final Map<W, W> mapping;\\n64: \\n65:     /**\\n66:      * Mapping when we created the {@code MergingWindowSet}. We use this to decide whether we need\\n67:      * to persist any changes to state.\\n68:      */\\n69:     private final Map<W, W> initialMapping;\\n70: \\n71:     private final ListState<Tuple2<W, W>> state;\\n72: \\n73:     /** Our window assigner. */\\n74:     private final MergingWindowAssigner<?, W> windowAssigner;\\n75: \\n76:     /** Restores a {@link MergingWindowSet} from the given state. */\\n77:     public MergingWindowSet(\\n78:             MergingWindowAssigner<?, W> windowAssigner, ListState<Tuple2<W, W>> state)\\n79:             throws Exception {\\n80:         this.windowAssigner = windowAssigner;\\n81:         mapping = new HashMap<>();\\n82: \\n83:         Iterable<Tuple2<W, W>> windowState = state.get();\\n84:         if (windowState != null) {\\n85:             for (Tuple2<W, W> window : windowState) {\\n86:                 mapping.put(window.f0, window.f1);\\n87:             }\\n88:         }\\n89: \\n90:         this.state = state;\\n91: \\n92:         initialMapping = new HashMap<>();\\n93:         initialMapping.putAll(mapping);\\n94:     }\\n95: \\n96:     /**\\n97:      * Persist the updated mapping to the given state if the mapping changed since initialization.\\n98:      */\\n99:     public void persist() throws Exception {\\n100:         if (!mapping.equals(initialMapping)) {\\n101:             state.update(\\n102:                     mapping.entrySet().stream()\\n103:                             .map((w) -> new Tuple2<>(w.getKey(), w.getValue()))\\n104:                             .collect(Collectors.toList()));\\n105:         }\\n106:     }\\n107: \\n108:     /**\\n109:      * Returns the state window for the given in-flight {@code Window}. The state window is the\\n110:      * {@code Window} in which we keep the actual state of a given in-flight window. Windows might\\n111:      * expand but we keep to original state window for keeping the elements of the window to avoid\\n112:      * costly state juggling.\\n113:      *\\n114:      * @param window The window for which to get the state window.\\n115:      */\\n116:     public W getStateWindow(W window) {\\n117:         return mapping.get(window);\\n118:     }\\n119: \\n120:     /**\\n121:      * Removes the given window from the set of in-flight windows.\\n122:      *\\n123:      * @param window The {@code Window} to remove.\\n124:      */\\n125:     public void retireWindow(W window) {\\n126:         W removed = this.mapping.remove(window);\\n127:         if (removed == null) {\\n128:             throw new IllegalStateException(\\n129:                     \\\"Window \\\" + window + \\\" is not in in-flight window set.\\\");\\n130:         }\\n131:     }\\n132: \\n133:     /**\\n134:      * Adds a new {@code Window} to the set of in-flight windows. It might happen that this triggers\\n135:      * merging of previously in-flight windows. In that case, the provided {@link MergeFunction} is\\n136:      * called.\\n137:      *\\n138:      * <p>This returns the window that is the representative of the added window after adding. This\\n139:      * can either be the new window itself, if no merge occurred, or the newly merged window. Adding\\n140:      * an element to a window or calling trigger functions should only happen on the returned\\n141:      * representative. This way, we never have to deal with a new window that is immediately\\n142:      * swallowed up by another window.\\n143:      *\\n144:      * <p>If the new window is merged, the {@code MergeFunction} callback arguments also don't\\n145:      * contain the new window as part of the list of merged windows.\\n146:      *\\n147:      * @param newWindow The new {@code Window} to add.\\n148:      * @param mergeFunction The callback to be invoked in case a merge occurs.\\n149:      * @return The {@code Window} that new new {@code Window} ended up in. This can also be the new\\n150:      *     {@code Window} itself in case no merge occurred.\\n151:      * @throws Exception\\n152:      */\\n153:     public W addWindow(W newWindow, MergeFunction<W> mergeFunction) throws Exception {\\n154: \\n155:         List<W> windows = new ArrayList<>();\\n156: \\n157:         windows.addAll(this.mapping.keySet());\\n158:         windows.add(newWindow);\\n159: \\n160:         final Map<W, Collection<W>> mergeResults = new HashMap<>();\\n161:         windowAssigner.mergeWindows(\\n162:                 windows,\\n163:                 new MergingWindowAssigner.MergeCallback<W>() {\\n164:                     @Override\\n165:                     public void merge(Collection<W> toBeMerged, W mergeResult) {\\n166:                         if (LOG.isDebugEnabled()) {\\n167:                             LOG.debug(\\\"Merging {} into {}\\\", toBeMerged, mergeResult);\\n168:                         }\\n169:                         mergeResults.put(mergeResult, toBeMerged);\\n170:                     }\\n171:                 });\\n172: \\n173:         W resultWindow = newWindow;\\n174:         boolean mergedNewWindow = false;\\n175: \\n176:         // perform the merge\\n177:         for (Map.Entry<W, Collection<W>> c : mergeResults.entrySet()) {\\n178:             W mergeResult = c.getKey();\\n179:             Collection<W> mergedWindows = c.getValue();\\n180: \\n181:             // if our new window is in the merged windows make the merge result the\\n182:             // result window\\n183:             if (mergedWindows.remove(newWindow)) {\\n184:                 mergedNewWindow = true;\\n185:                 resultWindow = mergeResult;\\n186:             }\\n187: \\n188:             // pick any of the merged windows and choose that window's state window\\n189:             // as the state window for the merge result\\n190:             W mergedStateWindow = this.mapping.get(mergedWindows.iterator().next());\\n191: \\n192:             // figure out the state windows that we are merging\\n193:             List<W> mergedStateWindows = new ArrayList<>();\\n194:             for (W mergedWindow : mergedWindows) {\\n195:                 W res = this.mapping.remove(mergedWindow);\\n196:                 if (res != null) {\\n197:                     mergedStateWindows.add(res);\\n198:                 }\\n199:             }\\n200: \\n201:             this.mapping.put(mergeResult, mergedStateWindow);\\n202: \\n203:             // don't put the target state window into the merged windows\\n204:             mergedStateWindows.remove(mergedStateWindow);\\n205: \\n206:             // don't merge the new window itself, it never had any state associated with it\\n207:             // i.e. if we are only merging one pre-existing window into itself\\n208:             // without extending the pre-existing window\\n209:             if (!(mergedWindows.contains(mergeResult) && mergedWindows.size() == 1)) {\\n210:                 mergeFunction.merge(\\n211:                         mergeResult,\\n212:                         mergedWindows,\\n213:                         this.mapping.get(mergeResult),\\n214:                         mergedStateWindows);\\n215:             }\\n216:         }\\n217: \\n218:         // the new window created a new, self-contained window without merging\\n219:         if (mergeResults.isEmpty() || (resultWindow.equals(newWindow) && !mergedNewWindow)) {\\n220:             this.mapping.put(resultWindow, resultWindow);\\n221:         }\\n222: \\n223:         return resultWindow;\\n224:     }\\n225: \\n226:     /**\\n227:      * Callback for {@link #addWindow(Window, MergeFunction)}.\\n228:      *\\n229:      * @param <W>\\n230:      */\\n231:     public interface MergeFunction<W> {\\n232: \\n233:         /**\\n234:          * This gets called when a merge occurs.\\n235:          *\\n236:          * @param mergeResult The newly resulting merged {@code Window}.\\n237:          * @param mergedWindows The merged {@code Window Windows}.\\n238:          * @param stateWindowResult The state window of the merge result.\\n239:          * @param mergedStateWindows The merged state windows.\\n240:          * @throws Exception\\n241:          */\\n242:         void merge(\\n243:                 W mergeResult,\\n244:                 Collection<W> mergedWindows,\\n245:                 W stateWindowResult,\\n246:                 Collection<W> mergedStateWindows)\\n247:                 throws Exception;\\n248:     }\\n249: \\n250:     @Override\\n251:     public String toString() {\\n252:         return \\\"MergingWindowSet{\\\" + Elements from the state windows of merged\\n42:  * windows must be merged into this one state window. We keep a mapping from in-flight window to\\n43:  * state window that can be queried using {@link #getStateWindow(Window)}.\\n44:  *\\n45:  * <p>A new window can be added to the set of in-flight windows using {@link #addWindow(Window,\\n46:  * MergeFunction)}. This might merge other windows and the caller must react accordingly in the\\n47:  * {@link MergeFunction#merge(Object, Collection, Object, Collection)} and adjust the outside view\\n48:  * of windows and state.\\n49:  *\\n50:  * <p>Windows can be removed from the set of windows using {@link #retireWindow(Window)}.\\n51:  *\\n52:  * @param <W> The type of {@code Window} that this set is keeping track of.\\n53:  */\\n54: public class MergingWindowSet<W extends Window> {\\n55: \\n56:     private static final Logger LOG = LoggerFactory.getLogger(MergingWindowSet.class);\\n57: \\n58:     /**\\n59:      * Mapping from window to the window that keeps the window state. When we are incrementally\\n60:      * merging windows starting from some window we keep that starting window as the state window to\\n61:      * prevent costly state juggling.\\n62:      */\\n63:     private final Map<W, W> mapping;\\n64: \\n65:     /**\\n66:      * Mapping when we created the {@code MergingWindowSet}. We use this to decide whether we need\\n67:      * to persist any changes to state.\\n68:      */\\n69:     private final Map<W, W> initialMapping;\\n70: \\n71:     private final ListState<Tuple2<W, W>> state;\\n72: \\n73:     /** Our window assigner. */\\n74:     private final MergingWindowAssigner<?, W> windowAssigner;\\n75: \\n76:     /** Restores a {@link MergingWindowSet} from the given state. */\\n77:     public MergingWindowSet(\\n78:             MergingWindowAssigner<?, W> windowAssigner, ListState<Tuple2<W, W>> state)\\n79:             throws Exception {\\n80:         this.windowAssigner = windowAssigner;\\n81:         mapping = new HashMap<>();\\n82: \\n83:         Iterable<Tuple2<W, W>> windowState = state.get();\\n84:         if (windowState != null) {\\n85:             for (Tuple2<W, W> window : windowState) {\\n86:                 mapping.put(window.f0, window.f1);\\n87:             }\\n88:         }\\n89: \\n90:         this.state = state;\\n91: \\n92:         initialMapping = new HashMap<>();\\n93:         initialMapping.putAll(mapping);\\n94:     }\\n95: \\n96:     /**\\n97:      * Persist the updated mapping to the given state if the mapping changed since initialization.\\n98:      */\\n99:     public void persist() throws Exception {\\n100:         if (!mapping.equals(initialMapping)) {\\n101:             state.update(\\n102:                     mapping.entrySet().stream()\\n103:                             .map((w) -> new Tuple2<>(w.getKey(), w.getValue()))\\n104:                             .collect(Collectors.toList()));\\n105:         }\\n106:     }\\n107: \\n108:     /**\\n109:      * Returns the state window for the given in-flight {@code Window}. The state window is the\\n110:      * {@code Window} in which we keep the actual state of a given in-flight window. Windows might\\n111:      * expand but we keep to original state window for keeping the elements of the window to avoid\\n112:      * costly state juggling.\\n113:      *\\n114:      * @param window The window for which to get the state window.\\n115:      */\\n116:     public W getStateWindow(W window) {\\n117:         return mapping.get(window);\\n118:     }\\n119: \\n120:     /**\\n121:      * Removes the given window from the set of in-flight windows.\\n122:      *\\n123:      * @param window The {@code Window} to remove.\\n124:      */\\n125:     public void retireWindow(W window) {\\n126:         W removed = this.mapping.remove(window);\\n127:         if (removed == null) {\\n128:             throw new IllegalStateException(\\n129:                     \\\"Window \\\" + window + \\\" is not in in-flight window set.\\\");\\n130:         }\\n131:     }\\n132: \\n133:     /**\\n134:      * Adds a new {@code Window} to the set of in-flight windows. It might happen that this triggers\\n135:      * merging of previously in-flight windows. In that case, the provided {@link MergeFunction} is\\n136:      * called.\\n137:      *\\n138:      * <p>This returns the window that is the representative of the added window after adding. This\\n139:      * can either be the new window itself, if no merge occurred, or the newly merged window. Adding\\n140:      * an element to a window or calling trigger functions should only happen on the returned\\n141:      * representative. This way, we never have to deal with a new window that is immediately\\n142:      * swallowed up by another window.\\n143:      *\\n144:      * <p>If the new window is merged, the {@code MergeFunction} callback arguments also don't\\n145:      * contain the new window as part of the list of merged windows.\\n146:      *\\n147:      * @param newWindow The new {@code Window} to add.\\n148:      * @param mergeFunction The callback to be invoked in case a merge occurs.\\n149:      * @return The {@code Window} that new new {@code Window} ended up in. This can also be the new\\n150:      *     {@code Window} itself in case no merge occurred.\\n151:      * @throws Exception\\n152:      */\\n153:     public W addWindow(W newWindow, MergeFunction<W> mergeFunction) throws Exception {\\n154: \\n155:         List<W> windows = new ArrayList<>();\\n156: \\n157:         windows.addAll(this.mapping.keySet());\\n158:         windows.add(newWindow);\\n159: \\n160:         final Map<W, Collection<W>> mergeResults = new HashMap<>();\\n161:         windowAssigner.mergeWindows(\\n162:                 windows,\\n163:                 new MergingWindowAssigner.MergeCallback<W>() {\\n164:                     @Override\\n165:                     public void merge(Collection<W> toBeMerged, W mergeResult) {\\n166:                         if (LOG.isDebugEnabled()) {\\n167:                             LOG.debug(\\\"Merging {} into {}\\\", toBeMerged, mergeResult);\\n168:                         }\\n169:                         mergeResults.put(mergeResult, toBeMerged);\\n170:                     }\\n171:                 });\\n172: \\n173:         W resultWindow = newWindow;\\n174:         boolean mergedNewWindow = false;\\n175: \\n176:         // perform the merge\\n177:         for (Map.Entry<W, Collection<W>> c : mergeResults.entrySet()) {\\n178:             W mergeResult = c.getKey();\\n179:             Collection<W> mergedWindows = c.getValue();\\n180: \\n181:             // if our new window is in the merged windows make the merge result the\\n182:             // result window\\n183:             if (mergedWindows.remove(newWindow)) {\\n184:                 mergedNewWindow = true;\\n185:                 resultWindow = mergeResult;\\n186:             }\\n187: \\n188:             // pick any of the merged windows and choose that window's state window\\n189:             // as the state window for the merge result\\n190:             W mergedStateWindow = this.mapping.get(mergedWindows.iterator().next());\\n191: \\n192:             // figure out the state windows that we are merging\\n193:             List<W> mergedStateWindows = new ArrayList<>();\\n194:             for (W mergedWindow : mergedWindows) {\\n195:                 W res = this.mapping.remove(mergedWindow);\\n196:                 if (res != null) {\\n197:                     mergedStateWindows.add(res);\\n198:                 }\\n199:             }\\n200: \\n201:             this.mapping.put(mergeResult, mergedStateWindow);\\n202: \\n203:             // don't put the target state window into the merged windows\\n204:             mergedStateWindows.remove(mergedStateWindow);\\n205: \\n206:             // don't merge the new window itself, it never had any state associated with it\\n207:             // i.e. if we are only merging one pre-existing window into itself\\n208:             // without extending the pre-existing window\\n209:             if (!(mergedWindows.contains(mergeResult) && mergedWindows.size() == 1)) {\\n210:                 mergeFunction.merge(\\n211:                         mergeResult,\\n212:                         mergedWindows,\\n213:                         this.mapping.get(mergeResult),\\n214:                         mergedStateWindows);\\n215:             }\\n216:         }\\n217: \\n218:         // the new mergedStateWindows)\\n247:                 throws Exception;\\n248:     }\\n249: \\n250:     @Override\\n251:     public String toString() {\\n252:         return \\\"MergingWindowSet{\\\" + When we are incrementally\n60:      * merging windows starting from some window we keep that starting window as the state window to\n61:      * prevent costly state juggling.\n62:      */\n63:     private final Map<W, W> mapping;\n64: \n65:     /**\n66:      * Mapping when we created the {@code MergingWindowSet}. The state window is the\n110:      * {@code Window} in which we keep the actual state of a given in-flight window. Windows might\n111:      * expand but we keep to original state window for keeping the elements of the window to avoid\n112:      * costly state juggling.\n113:      *\n114:      * @param window The window for which to get the state window.\n115:      */\n116:     public W getStateWindow(W window) {\n117:         return mapping.get(window);\n118:     }\n119: \n120:     /**\n121:      * Removes the given window from the set of in-flight windows.\n122:      *\n123:      * @param window The {@code Window} to remove.\n124:      */\n125:     public void retireWindow(W window) {\n126:         W removed = this.mapping.remove(window);\n127:         if (removed == null) {\n128:             throw new IllegalStateException(\n129:                     \"Window \" + window + \" is not in in-flight window set.\");\n130:         }\n131:     }\n132: \n133:     /**\n134:      * Adds a new {@code Window} to the set of in-flight windows. It might happen that this triggers\n135:      * merging of previously in-flight windows. In that case, the provided {@link MergeFunction} is\n136:      * called.\n137:      *\n138:      * <p>This returns the window that is the representative of the added window after adding. This\n139:      * can either be the new window itself, if no merge occurred, or the newly merged window. Adding\n140:      * an element to a window or calling trigger functions should only happen on the returned\n141:      * representative. This way, we never have to deal with a new window that is immediately\n142:      * swallowed up by another window.\n143:      *\n144:      * <p>If the new window is merged, the {@code MergeFunction} callback arguments also don't\n145:      * contain the new window as part of the list of merged windows.\n146:      *\n147:      * @param newWindow The new {@code Window} to add.\n148:      * @param mergeFunction The callback to be invoked in case a merge occurs.\n149:      * @return The {@code Window} that new new {@code Window} ended up in. This can also be the new\n150:      *     {@code Window} itself in case no merge occurred.\n151:      * @throws Exception\n152:      */\n153:     public W addWindow(W newWindow, MergeFunction<W> mergeFunction) throws Exception {\n154: \n155:         List<W> windows = new ArrayList<>();\n156: \n157:         windows.addAll(this.mapping.keySet());\n158:         windows.add(newWindow);\n159: \n160:         final Map<W, Collection<W>> mergeResults = new HashMap<>();\n161:         windowAssigner.mergeWindows(\n162:                 windows,\n163:                 new MergingWindowAssigner.MergeCallback<W>() {\n164:                     @Override\n165:                     public void merge(Collection<W> toBeMerged, W mergeResult) {\n166:                         if (LOG.isDebugEnabled()) {\n167:                             LOG.debug(\"Merging {} into {}\", toBeMerged, mergeResult);\n168:                         }\n169:                         mergeResults.put(mergeResult, toBeMerged);\n170:                     }\n171:                 });\n172: \n173:         W resultWindow = newWindow;\n174:         boolean mergedNewWindow = false;\n175: \n176:         // perform the merge\n177:         for (Map.Entry<W, Collection<W>> c : mergeResults.entrySet()) {\n178:             W mergeResult = c.getKey();\n179:             Collection<W> mergedWindows = c.getValue();\n180: \n181:             // if our new window is in the merged windows make the merge result the\n182:             // result window\n183:             if (mergedWindows.remove(newWindow)) {\n184:                 mergedNewWindow = true;\n185:                 resultWindow = mergeResult;\n186:             }\n187: \n188:             // pick any of the merged windows and choose that window's state window\n189:             // as the state window for the merge result\n190:             W mergedStateWindow = this.mapping.get(mergedWindows.iterator().next());\n191: \n192:             // figure out the state windows that we are merging\n193:             List<W> mergedStateWindows = new ArrayList<>();\n194:             for (W mergedWindow : mergedWindows) {\n195:                 W res = this.mapping.remove(mergedWindow);\n196:                 if (res != null) {\n197:                     mergedStateWindows.add(res);\n198:                 }\n199:             }\n200: \n201:             this.mapping.put(mergeResult, mergedStateWindow);\n202: \n203:             // don't put the target state window into the merged windows\n204:             mergedStateWindows.remove(mergedStateWindow);\n205: \n206:             // don't merge the new window itself, it never had any state associated with it\n207:             // i.e. if we are only merging one pre-existing window into itself\n208:             // without extending the pre-existing window\n209:             if (!(mergedWindows.contains(mergeResult) && mergedWindows.size() == 1)) {\n210:                 mergeFunction.merge(\n211:                         mergeResult,\n212:                         mergedWindows,\n213:                         this.mapping.get(mergeResult),\n214:                         mergedStateWindows);\n215:             }\n216:         }\n217: \n218:         // the new window created a new, self-contained window without merging\n219:         if (mergeResults.isEmpty() || (resultWindow.equals(newWindow) && !mergedNewWindow)) {\n220: {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01ALk66Q1dDf7LngvkePzcHj","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Good. Now I understand how `addWindow()` works:\n1. It takes the current set of in-flight windows from the mapping\n2. Adds the new window\n3. Calls the window assigner's `mergeWindows()` to determine what should be merged\n4. Performs the merge operations\n5. Returns the resulting window (either the new window if no merge, or the merged result)\n\nNow looking back at the WindowOperator code, I can see the bug more clearly:\n\nIn `processElement()` for merging windows (lines 303-403):\n```java\nif (windowAssigner instanceof MergingWindowAssigner) {\n    MergingWindowSet<W> mergingWindows = getMergingWindowSet();\n    \n    for (W window : elementWindows) {\n        // adding the new window might result in a merge\n        W actualWindow = mergingWindows.addWindow(window, ...);\n        \n        // drop if the window is already late\n        if (isWindowLate(actualWindow)) {\n            mergingWindows.retireWindow(actualWindow);\n            continue;\n        }\n        isSkippedElement = false;\n        // ... process element ...\n    }\n}\n```\n\nThe issue is:\n1. `addWindow()` can merge the new window with existing windows\n2. But then we check `isWindowLate(actualWindow)` which checks if the window's max timestamp is before the watermark\n3. For a late element that could merge into an existing window, the merged window might have a late max timestamp\n4. So we skip the element entirely\n\nActually, looking more carefully, let me think about when this happens:\n\nScenario:\n- Session window with 5-minute gap\n- allowedLateness = 0\n- Existing window in flight: [10:00, 10:05]\n- Element arrives with timestamp 10:04 (before the window closed)\n- Watermark = 10:10 (already passed the window end)\n\n\n- When the element is assigned to [10:04, 10:09] and merged with the existing window, it becomes [10:00, 10:09]. Since the watermark is at 10:10, this merged window is considered late and gets retired without processing the element. The element is dropped even though it arrived before the original window closed.\n- The core issue is that the late check happens after the merge, not before. The element should be checked against the original window boundaries first to determine if it could legitimately belong to an unmerged window before deciding it's too late.\n- The current logic marks a window as late based on cleanup time, but for merging windows, we need to verify if the element can actually merge into a window that's still active in memory, regardless of whether that window has technically passed its cleanup deadline.\n\nLooking at the merge function itself, there's a validation that prevents merging if the result window's end timestamp would fall before the current watermark. This check ensures we don't create windows with timestamps earlier than what's already been processed, which would violate the ordering guarantees.\n\nThe sequence is: when a new window is added, it checks for overlaps with existing windows, and if found, the merge function validates the resulting window's timestamp against the watermark. If the merge would produce a late window, an exception is thrown, which would propagate up and fail the entire `addWindow()` call. Looking at the merge function signature in MergingWindowSet, the merge operation is called with the result window and the collection of windows being merged together.\n\nThe issue is that `processElement()` in WindowOperator doesn't wrap the `addWindow()` call in exception handling, so any exception from the merge validation would crash the whole method. The late window check itself—comparing the merge result's max timestamp plus allowed lateness against the current watermark—is meant to prevent backwards-in-time merges, but it doesn't address the core problem we're investigating.\n\nLooking at this more carefully, the actual bug is about late-arriving data being silently dropped instead of sent to side outputs when session windows are involved. Tracing the flow: for merging windows, after `addWindow()` potentially combines windows, the result gets checked with `isWindowLate()`, and if it's late, the window gets retired and the loop moves on. Only after processing all windows does the code check if `isSkippedElement` and `isElementLate()` are both true to emit to the side output. For non-merging windows, the window is checked immediately and the loop continues if it's late. The difference in how these paths handle late data is where the problem likely lies. merge with. Let me reconsider the flow: when a late element arrives, `addWindow()` creates a new window and merges it with existing windows. If the resulting merged window is late, we skip processing it entirely. But this means we're discarding the element even though it could have been added to an existing non-late window during the merge. The real issue is that we need to check if the element can merge with an existing window before deciding to skip it—if it can merge with a non-late window, we should allow that merge to happen rather than treating it as late data. the lateness check happens before considering whether the element could extend an existing window through merging. Let me trace through the actual code flow: `addWindow()` is called first to handle the merge, then `isWindowLate()` checks if the result is late. But the bug description suggests the lateness determination should account for whether merging is possible before rejecting the element as late.\n\nLooking at the code paths for merging windows, if all resulting windows are marked late, the element gets skipped. Then at line 440, the code either sends it to a side output or increments the dropped counter—so there shouldn't be silent drops. The files are in the remote Sourcegraph repository. I need to download/clone the repository first or work with the MCP tools to read and understand the code, then create/edit local files.\n\nBased on the bug description and the code I've read so far, let me analyze what the actual bug is:\n\nLooking at WindowOperator.processElement() again:\n\nFor merging windows (lines 303-403):\n```java\nif (windowAssigner instanceof MergingWindowAssigner) {\n    MergingWindowSet<W> mergingWindows = getMergingWindowSet();\n\n    for (W window : elementWindows) {\n        W actualWindow = mergingWindows.addWindow(window, new MergingWindowSet.MergeFunction<W>() {...});\n        \n        // drop if the window is already late\n        if (isWindowLate(actualWindow)) {\n            mergingWindows.retireWindow(actualWindow);\n            continue;\n        }\n        isSkippedElement = false;\n        // ... process element ...\n    }\n    mergingWindows.persist();\n}\n```\n\nFor non-merging windows (lines 405-433):\n```java\n} else {\n    for (W window : elementWindows) {\n        // drop if the window is already late\n        if (isWindowLate(window)) {\n            continue;\n        }\n        isSkippedElement = false;\n        // ... process element ...\n    }\n}\n```\n\nThen for late data side output (lines 440-446):\n```java\nif (isSkippedElement && isElementLate(element)) {\n    if (lateDataOutputTag != null) {\n        sideOutput(element);\n    } else {\n        this.numLateRecordsDropped.inc();\n    }\n}\n```\n\nNow, I think I finally understand the bug! Looking at the two cases:\n\n1. **Non-merging windows**: If a window is late (isWindowLate returns true), we just continue to the next window, but we DON'T retire the window or do anything with the merging logic. \n\nNon-merging windows don't require merge logic, so this is expected behavior.\n\n2. **Merging windows**: If a window is late (isWindowLate returns true), we call `mergingWindows.retireWindow(actualWindow)` and continue. But here's the issue: by retiring the window without processing the element, we might be losing data that could have been merged with an existing window!\n\nBut wait, that still doesn't fully explain the \"silently dropped\" issue. Let me think about this differently...\n\nActually, I think the issue might be subtler. Let me trace through a concrete example:\n\n**Scenario:**\n- Session window with 5-minute gap\n- allowedLateness = 10 minutes\n- An element arrives that's 8 minutes late, which should still be processed since it's within the allowed lateness window. The element could potentially merge with an existing session, but if isWindowLate() returns true based on some other criteria, we retire the window and skip processing entirely. This means the element never gets a chance to merge, and it's silently dropped without being sent to the late data side output because isSkippedElement remains true but the element itself isn't flagged as late by isElementLate(). merge) - `isWindowLate([10:04, 10:09])` checks if 10:09 + 0 <= 10:10, which is true, so the window gets retired and element 2 is skipped. Then `isElementLate(element2)` checks if 10:04 + 0 <= 10:10, also true, meaning element 2 should go to the side output. The logic appears sound, but based on the bug description, the real problem seems to be that when `sideOutput()` is invoked, the output collector doesn't have the tag registered, so the element silently disappears instead of being routed correctly.\n\nThe `sideOutput` method just calls `output.collect(lateDataOutputTag, element)`, which would drop the element if the tag isn't registered. However, I suspect the actual issue is different - we're determining lateness before checking whether an element could actually merge with an existing window. An element arriving after the watermark might still extend a session, but we're rejecting it as late too early in the process.\n\nLooking more carefully at the code flow, `addWindow()` handles merging at lines 311-366, and this happens before the lateness check at line 369. So the window should already be merged if applicable by the time we evaluate whether it's late. That means the problem isn't about the order of operations there. I should examine the `EvictingWindowOperator` to see if it has a different approach to this scenario.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":8,"cache_creation_input_tokens":289,"cache_read_input_tokens":71269,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":289},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"518f637c-6365-41be-ba92-25ff733d5222","uuid":"806fee96-24d5-469b-9d5b-15a6949011a1"}

Session windows do not overlap and\\n318: do not have a fixed start and end time, in contrast to *tumbling windows* and *sliding windows*. Instead a\\n319: session window closes when it does not receive elements for a certain period of time, *i.e.*, when a gap of\\n320: inactivity occurred. A session window assigner can be configured with either a static *session gap* or with a \\n321: *session gap extractor* function which defines how long the period of inactivity is. When this period expires, \\n322: the current session closes and subsequent elements are assigned to a new session window.\\n323: \\n324: {{< img src=\\\"/fig/session-windows.svg\\\" alt=\\\"session windows\\\" >}} \\n325: \\n326: The following code snippets show how to use session windows.\\n327: \\n328: \",\"endLine\":328,\"startLine\":314},{\"content\":\"1193: \\n1194: ## Allowed Lateness\\n1195: \\n1196: When working with *event-time* windowing, it can happen that elements arrive late, *i.e.* the watermark that Flink uses to\\n1197: keep track of the progress of event-time is already past the end timestamp of a window to which an element belongs. \n1201: By default, late elements are dropped when the watermark is past the end of the window. However,\n1202: Flink allows to specify a maximum *allowed lateness* for window operators. Allowed lateness\n1203: specifies by how much time elements can be late before they are dropped, and its default value is 0.\n1204: Elements that arrive after the watermark has passed the end of the window but before it passes the end of\n1205: the window plus the allowed lateness, are still added to the window. Depending on the trigger used,\n1206: a late but not dropped element may cause the window to fire again. This is the case for the `EventTimeTrigger`.\n1207: \n1208: In order to make this work, Flink keeps the state of windows until their allowed lateness expires. Once this happens, Flink removes the window and deletes its state, as\n1209: also described in the [Window Lifecycle](#window-lifecycle) section.\n1210: \n1211: ... [chunk truncated]","endLine":1215,"startLine":1200},{"content":"314: \n315: ### Session Windows\n316: \n317: The *session windows* assigner groups elements by sessions of activity. Session windows do not overlap and\n318: do not have a fixed start and end time, in contrast to *tumbling windows* and *sliding windows*. Instead a\n319: session window closes when it does not receive elements for a certain period of time, *i.e.*, when a gap of\n320: inactivity occurred. A session window assigner can be configured with either a static *session gap* or with a \n321: *session gap extractor* function which defines how long the period of inactivity is. When this period expires, \n322: the current session closes and subsequent elements are assigned to a new session window.\n323: \n324: {{< img src=\"/fig/session-windows.svg\" alt=\"session windows\" >}} \n325: \n326: The following code snippets show how to use session windows.\n327: \n328: ","endLine":328,"startLine":314},{"content":"1193: \n1194: ## Allowed Lateness\n1195: \n1196: When working with *event-time* windowing, it can happen that elements arrive late, *i.e.* the watermark that Flink uses to\n1197: keep track of the progress of event-time is already past the end timestamp of a window to which an element belongs. The test passes, which means the feature works. But the task description says there's a bug that needs to be fixed. Maybe the issue is not in the current code but is a theoretical scenario that could happen under certain conditions.\n\nLet me re-read the bug description once more and think about when the bug could occur:\n\n\"An element arrives with a timestamp past the current watermark\"- In the test, the element with timestamp 10000 arrives when the watermark is 14600.\n- So the element is 4600ms late (past the watermark).\n- \"past the current watermark\" in this context means \"before the watermark\" (i.e., a late element).\n\n\"For merging windows, this element could have been merged into an existing window that hasn't been cleaned up yet\"\n- The element with timestamp 10000 could potentially merge with a window that exists.\n- But in this test, all windows have already fired and been cleaned up by the time the element arrives.\n\n\"The late element handling code path attempts `sideOutput()` but the output collector may not have the tag registered, causing the element to be silently dropped\"\n- This suggests that the element is not being sent to the side output, or the tag is not properly registered.\n\nActually, now I'm wondering if the issue is something different. Let me think about what could go wrong...\n\nActually, I just realized something! What if the bug is in the case where an element could merge with an existing window, but the merged window is checked for lateness AFTER the merge, and it's determined to be late, so we retire it without processing the element?\n\n\nLooking at the code flow, when an element merges with an existing window and the resulting merged window is determined to be late, the code retires the window and continues without sending the element to the side output. The element should still be routed to the side output even when the merged window is late, but currently it's being skipped entirely.\n\nThe real issue appears to be in how `isElementLate()` is evaluated at the end. If this method returns false—perhaps because the element's timestamp falls within the allowed lateness window—then even though `isSkippedElement` is true, the condition to send it to the side output won't trigger. This could happen if the allowedLateness configuration is non-zero and the element hasn't exceeded that threshold, even though it arrived after the watermark.\n\nAlternatively, the problem might stem from retiring windows prematurely in the merging window case. When `isWindowLate()` triggers a retirement, it could prevent subsequent elements from extending that window, which defeats the purpose of allowing late data in merging scenarios. But first I need to check the actual code more carefully. Let me look at what happens when a late element arrives and could merge with an existing window.\n\nActually, I think I need to understand the scenario better. Let me trace through it step by step:\n\n1. Element with timestamp 1000 arrives\n   - Watermark is 0\n   - Element is assigned to window [1000, 1005]\n   - Window is not late (1005 <= 0 is false)\n   - Element is added to the window\n   - isSkippedElement = false\n   - Trigger fires, window fires\n\n2. Watermark advances to 1010\n   - Window [1000, 1005] has cleanup time 1005 + 0 = 1005\n   - Is 1005 <= 1010? Yes, so window is late and cleaned up\n\n3. Element with timestamp 1003 arrives\n   - Watermark is 1010\n   - Element is assigned to window [1003, 1008]\n   - addWindow() is called\n   - No existing windows to merge with (the first window was already cleaned up)\n   - So actualWindow = [1003, 1008]\n   - isWindowLate([1003, 1008])? Check: 1008 <= 1010? Yes, window is late\n   - Retire the window and continue\n   - No other windows to process\n   - isSkippedElement = true\n   - isElementLate() checks: 1003 + 0 <= 1010? Yes\n   - lateDataOutputTag != null, so sideOutput() is called\n\nSo in this case, the element SHOULD be sent to side output. But let me think about a different scenario...What if the first element hasn't fired yet when the second element arrives?\n\nLet me create a better test case:\n\n1. Element with timestamp 1000 arrives, watermark is still 0\n   - Window [1000, 1005] is created and element is added\n   - Trigger fires but window hasn't emitted yet\n\n2. Watermark advances to 1002 (before window cleanup time)\n   - Window remains active\n\n3. Element with timestamp 1001 arrives, watermark is still 1002\n   - Element gets assigned to window [1001, 1006]\n   - addWindow() merges with [1000, 1005], creating [1000, 1006]\n   - Check if merged window is late: 1006 + 0 <= 1002? No, so it's not late and continues processing normally\n\n4. Watermark advances to 1010\n   - Window [1000, 1006] cleanup time is 1006\n   - Since 1006 <= 1010, window gets cleaned up\n\n5. Element with timestamp 1005 arrives, watermark is 1010\n   - Assigned to window [1005, 1010] with no existing windows to merge\n   - Late check: 1010 + 0 <= 1010? Yes, so window is marked late and retired\n   - Element is also late: 1005 + 0 <= 1010? Yes\n   - Since lateDataOutputTag is set, element goes to side output\n\nThe element should reach the side output, so the issue must be elsewhere in the logic.\n\nLet me consider a different case: what if an element could have merged with an existing window, but that window was already cleaned up? Say an element with timestamp 1004 arrives after the watermark hits 1010. The original window [1000, 1005] would have been cleaned up, so the element gets assigned to [1004, 1009] instead. This new window is late since 1009 <= 1010, triggering retirement and the late element check.\n\nThe element passes through isElementLate() and reaches the side output code. But re-reading the bug description carefully—it mentions \"merging windows\" and \"an existing window that hasn't been cleaned up yet,\" which means the window still exists. The issue is that the side output handling tries to call `sideOutput()` but the tag isn't registered. This suggests the element does reach that code path, but something fails there. Maybe when retiring the window with `mergingWind`\n\nows.retireWindow(actualWindow)`, there's an interaction with the output collector that breaks things, or perhaps the element never reaches the side output code at all.\n\nLooking at the task again—I need to write a test that verifies late elements actually appear in the side output stream for session windows with `sideOutputLateData(tag)`. If the test fails, it means the late element is being silently dropped instead of being sent to the side output.\n\nThe issue must be that late elements aren't reaching the side output. When I trace through the window processing logic, if all windows for an element are late, `isSkippedElement` stays `true` since it's only set to `false` when a non-late window is processed. This means the element might not trigger the side output logic at all, or there's a condition preventing it from being sent to the side output even when it should be. The two methods are evaluating different conditions. `isElementLate()` compares the element's timestamp against the watermark, while `isWindowLate()` checks the window's end time. For a late element with timestamp 1003 arriving when the watermark is 1010 and allowedLateness is 0, the element would be marked late. If it merges with an existing window spanning [1000, 1005], the resulting window [1000, 1008] would also be considered late since 1008 + 0 ≤ 1010, triggering window retirement. The element should then be routed to the side output.\n\nLooking at the non-merging windows implementation reveals a critical difference: when a window is already late, the code simply skips it with `continue` rather than retiring it. In contrast, the merging windows path calls `mergingWindows.retireWindow(` when encountering a late window, which appears to be where the behavior diverges.\n\nBut examining the end of `processElement()` for both cases shows identical logic—both check `if (isSkippedElement && isElementLate(element))` and route to side output or increment the dropped counter accordingly. This suggests the issue isn't in the final routing logic itself, but rather in how `isSkippedElement` gets set or how the window retirement affects downstream behavior.\n\nLet me step back and trace through the actual execution flow more carefully. I should verify the existing test passes first, then construct a test case that exposes the problem. Re-reading the bug description more precisely: \"checks lateness BEFORE merging\" likely means the lateness evaluation happens before `addWindow()` executes the merge operation. The lateness check itself appears to occur at the end of the method, but perhaps the window state or merge behavior changes what gets classified as late.\n\nThe real issue might be about the order of operations: when an element arrives and could extend an existing window through merging, we need to determine whether to process it based on whether it can actually merge with something, not just whether the resulting window would be considered late. If we're rejecting elements that could validly extend existing windows because the merged result would be late, that's the bug.\n\nThe fix likely involves checking whether a window is a merge result before applying the lateness check—if existing windows were involved in the merge, the element should be processed regardless of whether the combined window exceeds the allowed lateness threshold.\n\nTo detect if a merge occurred, I can compare the actual window returned from `addWindow()` against the new window being added. If they differ, a merge happened. This means checking `actualWindow.equals(window)` would indicate whether the window was modified through merging, allowing me to skip the late check in those cases and only apply it to genuinely new windows. as a merge, we don't skip it even if the merged window is late. Looking at the MergingWindowSet logic, when `mergeResults.isEmpty()` or the new window wasn't merged into anything, the result window maps to itself. But if a merge occurs, the result window differs from the new window or the merge flag is true, meaning the window is extending existing ones and should be processed regardless of lateness.\n\nIn WindowOperator, I can distinguish between these cases by checking if `actualWindow.equals(window)`. When they're equal, no merge happened and we can skip late windows. When they differ, a merge occurred and we should process the element since it's extending an existing window. Though there's still the question of whether a late merged window should be skipped anyway.\n\nFrom a user's perspective, if a session window with a 5-second gap receives an element that would extend an existing window—say an element at timestamp 1003 extending a window from [1000, 1005] to [1000, 1008]—should it be processed or skipped? The bug suggests it should be processed since it merged with an existing window, but extending the window also extends the cleanup time, which raises questions about the semantics. The core issue seems to be understanding what \"merging windows\" means for session windows—when an element falls within the gap of an existing window, it merges with that window.\n\nIf a watermark has already passed the original window's end time, the new element arriving at 1003 would still extend the window. Since the user configured allowedLateness, this element should be processed despite the watermark being ahead. The fix likely involves checking whether a window is the result of a merge operation before deciding to skip it based on lateness.\n\nWhen merging occurs, the actualWindow differs from the original window. So the logic should allow processing if merging happened—the element is extending an existing window rather than creating a brand new late one. The check becomes: only retire the window if it's genuinely late AND no merge took place. If actualWindow equals window, it's a new late window and should be skipped. Otherwise, the merge is extending an existing window and should proceed.\n\nTesting this against scenarios: an element arriving after watermark cleanup creates a new late window that gets skipped. An element arriving before cleanup merges with an existing window, making actualWindow different from window, so it processes normally. In the boundary case where an element might merge with a window near the cleanup threshold, the merge detection prevents incorrectly skipping valid extended windows.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":4,"cache_creation_input_tokens":2656,"cache_read_input_tokens":109872,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":2656},"output_tokens":2,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"518f637c-6365-41be-ba92-25ff733d5222","uuid":"fd9c3108-6143-4fb9-85b4-2889df2c59a3"}
